---
title: "Linear Systems, Matrix Exponentials, and Modes"
description: "How first-order linear systems evolve through matrices, why the matrix exponential is the exact continuous-time evolution operator, and how eigenvectors reveal the modes that grow, decay, or oscillate."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: odes-and-dynamical-systems
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/second-order-systems-state-variables-and-reduction-to-first-order.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain what a linear time-invariant system y' = Ay is."
  - "Understand the matrix exponential as the exact evolution map for y' = Ay."
  - "Recognize eigenvectors and eigenvalues as dynamical modes."
  - "Connect modal structure to growth, decay, oscillation, and coupling."
applications:
  - engineering-systems
  - control-and-dynamics
  - scientific-computing
keywords:
  - linear systems
  - matrix exponential
  - modes
  - eigenvalues
  - state space
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/second-order-systems-state-variables-and-reduction-to-first-order.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/matrix-analysis/concepts/spectral-inequalities-and-variational-principles.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.03 Differential Equations"
  - "MIT 18.03SC differential equations"
  - "Stanford ENGR155A Ordinary Differential Equations for Engineers"
  - "Stanford MATH63CM Modern Mathematics: Continuous Methods"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.03SC Unit IV first-order systems"
  - "MIT 18.03SC linear systems introduction"
  - "MIT 18.03SC matrix exponentials"
  - "MIT 18.03SC matrix methods eigenvalues and normal modes"
  - "Stanford ENGR155A bulletin"
  - "Stanford MATH63CM bulletin"
---

## Role

This is the third page of the `ODEs and Dynamical Systems` module.

Its job is to show how the state-space rewrite from the previous page becomes a genuine linear-algebra story:

`matrix multiplication gives the local rule, and the matrix exponential gives the exact flow`

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Second-Order Systems, State Variables, and Reduction to First Order](second-order-systems-state-variables-and-reduction-to-first-order.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- what a linear time-invariant system `y' = Ay` is
- why `e^{tA}` is the exact continuous-time evolution operator
- how eigenvectors and eigenvalues become dynamical modes
- why coupling can often be understood by changing coordinates to modal variables

## Why It Matters

Once a model is written in state-space form, linear systems are the first major tractable class.

They appear in:

- oscillators and coupled mechanical systems
- circuits and filters
- local models near equilibria
- control and state estimation
- numerical time stepping and stability analysis
- diffusion, flow, and continuous-depth models in ML

The key step is to stop thinking only in coordinates and start thinking in operators:

- the matrix `A` tells us the local rule for change
- the matrix exponential `e^{tA}` tells us the exact finite-time evolution
- eigenvectors tell us the preferred directions of motion
- eigenvalues tell us whether those directions grow, decay, or oscillate

This is where linear algebra becomes dynamics instead of only static transformation.

## Prerequisite Recall

- the previous page rewrote second-order equations as first-order state-space systems
- eigenvalues and eigenvectors describe invariant directions of a matrix
- diagonalization is useful when a matrix has enough independent eigenvectors
- numerical methods later approximate flows of systems, but here we are describing the exact linear flow

## Intuition

### A Linear System Evolves A Whole State Vector

A linear time-invariant system has the form

$$
y'(t)=Ay(t),
$$

where `y(t)` is a vector and `A` is a constant matrix.

The derivative of the whole state is determined by multiplying the current state by `A`.

### The Matrix Exponential Is The Right Exact Analogue Of `e^{\\lambda t}`

For the scalar equation

$$
x'=\lambda x,
$$

the solution is

$$
x(t)=e^{\lambda t}x(0).
$$

The matrix exponential is the system version of that formula:

$$
y(t)=e^{tA}y(0).
$$

### Modes Decompose Complicated Motion Into Simpler Pieces

If `v` is an eigenvector of `A` with eigenvalue `\lambda`, then motion started exactly in the direction `v` stays in that direction and evolves like

$$
e^{\lambda t}v.
$$

That is a mode.

So each eigenpair gives a basic pattern:

- real negative eigenvalue: decay
- real positive eigenvalue: growth
- purely imaginary or complex eigenvalues: oscillation or rotation

### Coupling Often Looks Harder Than It Is

In the original coordinates, variables may appear strongly coupled.

But if we move to modal coordinates, the dynamics often decouple or nearly decouple.

That is why eigenvectors matter dynamically, not just algebraically.

## Formal Core

::: {#def-linear-system}
## Definition: Linear Time-Invariant System

A linear time-invariant first-order system has the form

$$
y'(t)=Ay(t)+r(t),
$$

where `A` is a constant matrix and `r(t)` is a forcing term.
:::

The homogeneous case is

$$
y'(t)=Ay(t).
$$

::: {#def-matrix-exponential}
## Definition: Matrix Exponential

For a square matrix `A`, the matrix exponential is defined by the power series

$$
e^{tA} = I + tA + \frac{t^2}{2!}A^2 + \frac{t^3}{3!}A^3 + \cdots
$$
:::

This is the system-level version of the scalar exponential.

::: {#thm-homogeneous-linear}
## Theorem Idea: Exact Solution Of A Homogeneous Linear System

For

$$
y'(t)=Ay(t), \qquad y(0)=y_0,
$$

the exact solution is

$$
y(t)=e^{tA}y_0.
$$
:::

This is the load-bearing fact behind linear systems.

::: {#thm-mode}
## Theorem Idea: Eigenvectors Produce Modes

If

$$
Av=\lambda v,
$$

then

$$
y(t)=e^{\lambda t}v
$$

is a solution of the homogeneous system `y' = Ay`.
:::

So eigenvectors define invariant directions and eigenvalues define the time behavior along those directions.

::: {#thm-diagonalizable}
## Theorem Idea: Diagonalization Turns Coupled Dynamics Into Modal Dynamics

If `A = V\Lambda V^{-1}` is diagonalizable, then

$$
e^{tA}=V e^{t\Lambda} V^{-1},
$$

and the system can be understood by evolving each modal coordinate separately.
:::

This is why modal decomposition is one of the cleanest first lenses for linear systems.

## A Small Worked Example

Consider the system

$$
y'(t)=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 \\
-2 & -3
\end{bmatrix}
y(t).
$$

This is exactly the state-space form of the second-order equation

$$
x''+3x'+2x=0.
$$

### Step 1: Find The Eigenvalues

The characteristic polynomial is

$$
\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = (\lambda+1)(\lambda+2),
$$

so the eigenvalues are

$$
\lambda_1=-1,\qquad \lambda_2=-2.
$$

### Step 2: Read The Modes

Each eigenvalue gives a decaying exponential mode:

- one decays like `e^{-t}`
- the other decays like `e^{-2t}`

So every solution is a combination of two decaying modes.

### Step 3: Read The Qualitative Dynamics

Because both eigenvalues are negative:

- trajectories move toward the origin
- there is no sustained oscillation
- one mode decays faster than the other, so long-time behavior is eventually dominated by the slower mode `e^{-t}`

Even without writing the full closed-form solution, the modal picture already tells us what the system does.

## Computation Lens

The matrix exponential is the exact finite-time flow of a linear system.

Numerical solvers such as Euler, Runge-Kutta, or exponential integrators are trying to approximate that exact flow.

This is why linear systems are such a useful benchmark:

- exact evolution is available in principle through `e^{tA}`
- numerical methods can be compared against that exact linear benchmark
- spectral structure of `A` controls stiffness, transient decay, and long-time behavior

This page therefore sits directly between exact dynamics and [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](../../numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd).

## Application Lens

### Oscillators And Coupled Systems

Mass-spring and circuit models become matrix systems once several state variables evolve together.

### Control And State-Space Models

Modern control begins with linear systems because modal structure, reachability, and stability are easiest to analyze there.

### Continuous-Time ML

Linear flows are the simplest continuous-depth models, and they also provide the local template used to analyze nonlinear systems near equilibria.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- what a linear system `y' = Ay` is
- why `e^{tA}` is the exact evolution operator
- why eigenvectors are modes
- why eigenvalues determine growth, decay, and oscillation

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

After this page, the next natural step is:

- [Phase Portraits, Equilibria, and Local Stability](phase-portraits-equilibria-and-local-stability.qmd)

The strongest adjacent pages are:

- [ODEs and Dynamical Systems](../index.qmd)
- [Second-Order Systems, State Variables, and Reduction to First Order](second-order-systems-state-variables-and-reduction-to-first-order.qmd)
- [Phase Portraits, Equilibria, and Local Stability](phase-portraits-equilibria-and-local-stability.qmd)
- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](../../numerical-methods/concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)
- [Eigenvalues and Diagonalization](../../../foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd)
- [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](../../numerical-methods/concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [MIT 18.03SC Unit IV: First-order Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/) - official unit page covering linear systems, phase portraits, matrix methods, and linearization. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linear Systems Introduction](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linear-systems-introduction/) - official introduction to first-order linear systems and matrix notation. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Matrix Exponentials](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/matrix-exponentials/) - official course page for the matrix exponential and exact evolution of linear systems. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Matrix Methods: Eigenvalues and Normal Modes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/matrix-methods-eigenvalues-and-normal-modes/) - official course page tying eigenvalues directly to modal behavior. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR155A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043152) - official engineering ODE course description connecting second-order equations, systems, and numerical methods. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MATH63CM bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1172621) - official proof-based ODE course description emphasizing linear systems and asymptotic behavior. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.03SC Unit IV: First-order Systems](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/) - `First pass` - official system-level ODE unit showing how linear systems sit inside the larger dynamics story. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Linear Systems Introduction](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/linear-systems-introduction/) - `First pass` - official introduction to state-space linear systems. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Matrix Exponentials](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/matrix-exponentials/) - `First pass` - official course page for the exact evolution operator of linear systems. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.03SC Matrix Methods: Eigenvalues and Normal Modes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-03sc-differential-equations-fall-2011/pages/unit-iv-first-order-systems/matrix-methods-eigenvalues-and-normal-modes/) - `First pass` - official page tying eigenvectors and eigenvalues to modal structure. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford ENGR155A bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1043152) - `Second pass` - official engineering course description linking second-order equations, systems, and numerics. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford MATH63CM bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1172621) - `Second pass` - official proof-based course description emphasizing linear systems and stability. Checked `2026-04-25`.
